Start from the "Start" node and follow the arrows to each subsequent node. For decision nodes (diamonds), evaluate the condition and follow the appropriate branch. Continue until you reach the "End" node.

Great! Now let me query the latest version information for React using the most relevant library ID.

### Install React 18 with npm and yarn

Source: https://react.dev/blog/2022/03/08/react-18-upgrade-guide

Commands to install the latest version of React 18 and React DOM packages. Use npm install or yarn add depending on your package manager preference.

```bash
npm install react react-dom
```

```bash
yarn add react react-dom
```

--------------------------------

### Check Installed React Version

Source: https://react.dev/reference/react-compiler/target

Verifies the installed React version in the project to ensure the compiler target matches the actual React version being used.

```bash
npm why react
```

### React Versions

Source: https://react.dev/versions

The React docs at [react.dev](https://react.dev) provide documentation for the latest version of React. We aim to keep the docs updated within major versions, and do not publish versions for each minor or patch version. When a new major is released, we archive the docs for the previous version as `x.react.dev`. See our [versioning policy](/community/versioning-policy) for more info.

--------------------------------

### All release channels > Latest channel

Source: https://react.dev/community/versioning-policy

Latest is the channel used for stable React releases and corresponds to the `latest` tag on npm. It is the recommended channel for all React apps that are shipped to real users. If you're not sure which channel you should use, it's Latest. If you're using React directly, this is what you're already using. You can expect updates to Latest to be extremely stable, with versions following the semantic versioning scheme.

--------------------------------

### The Next Major Version of React > React 19 Release

Source: https://react.dev/blog/2024/02/15/react-labs-what-we-have-been-working-on-february-2024

After years of iteration, `react@canary` is now ready to ship to `react@latest` as React 19, a new major version. The new features are compatible with any environment an app runs in and provide everything needed for production use. Since Asset Loading and Document Metadata may be breaking changes for some apps, the next version of React will be a major version release that also includes long-requested improvements like support for Web Components.

Perfect! Here's a comprehensive summary of what I found:

## Daily Task Workflow Structure

The **daily-task-workflow** follows a branching workflow with the following structure:

```
Start
  ↓
Ask User Question (What task do you want?)
  ├─→ "news" branch
  │    ↓
  │    Sub-Agent: news_briefing_agent
  │    ↓
  │    Readout News Report
  │    ↓
  │    End
  │
  └─→ "daily report" branch
       ↓
       Enter your prompt template
       ↓
       End
```

**Key Components:**
- **Start Node**: Initial entry point
- **Decision Node**: Asks the user what task they want (accepts "news" or "daily report")
- **Two Execution Paths**:
  1. **News Path**: Routes to a news_briefing_agent sub-agent that generates and readouts a news report
  2. **Daily Report Path**: Accepts a custom prompt template for generating a daily report
- **End Node**: Workflow conclusion point

---

## React Library Resolution Results

**Selected Library ID:** `/websites/react_dev`

This is the most comprehensive option with:
- **2,796 Code Snippets** (highest among React options)
- **High Source Reputation** (official React documentation)
- **Benchmark Score: 93.5** (highest quality score)
- **Description**: Official React documentation for building UIs with reusable components

---

## React Latest Version Information

Based on the documentation query:

- **Current Stable Version**: React 18 (with React 19 released)
- **Installation Command**:
  ```bash
  npm install react react-dom
  # or
  yarn add react react-dom
  ```
- **Version Check**: Use `npm why react` to verify your installed version
- **Release Channel**: The "Latest" channel on npm provides stable, production-ready releases following semantic versioning
- **React 19**: Recently released with major improvements including Web Components support, Asset Loading, and Document Metadata features
